front: Composer: married Clara Wieck in 1840 (his 'Year of Song'); expressed himself through fictional alter egos Florestan (fiery) and Eusebius (lyrical); co-founded the Neue Zeitschrift für Musik

Composer: championed Chopin with Eusebius exclaiming 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius!' in a review of Chopin's variations on 'Là ci darem la mano'; published 'New Paths' endorsing the young Brahms in 1853

Composer: attempted suicide by jumping into the Rhine; died at the Endenich asylum (1856); his last piece, Ghost Variations, was based on a melody he thought was dictated by ghosts but was from his own D minor Violin Concerto

Étude: set of 21 piano miniatures on an ASCH cryptogram; depicts commedia dell'arte characters alongside 'Chiarina' (Clara), 'Estrella,' Paganini, and Chopin; contains a never-to-be-played 'Sphinxes' section; ends with a 'March of the Davidsbündler'
Song Cycle: 16 Heine songs; opens with 'Im wunderschönen Monat Mai' (ends on an unresolved dominant 7th); 'Ich grolle nicht' (C major chords); last song imagines casting a coffin into the sea
Étude: 13 short piano pieces depicting a child's world; most famous movement is 'Träumerei' ('Dreaming')
Étude: piano fantasy in C major; first movement has an 'Im Legenden-ton' middle section and ends with a coda quoting Beethoven's An die ferne Geliebte; originally titled 'Ruins'

Concerto: in A minor; premiered by Clara Wieck; begins with a tutti chord, three bars of solo piano, two more tutti chords, and a woodwind chorale from the oboe; main theme contains a cipher for 'Chiara'

Concerto: E-flat major chamber work; first movement opens with a minor-seventh leap (E-flat to D-flat); second movement is a funeral march; finale has a double fugue; influenced Brahms's Piano Quintet
Symphony: 'Rhenish' symphony in E-flat; five movements; inspired by Cologne Cathedral; fourth movement features the trombones' first entrance — a solemn chorale in E-flat minor
Symphony: C major symphony — the only homotonal 19th-century symphony (all movements in C); Mendelssohn conducted the premiere; second-movement scherzo unusually has two trios
Composer: two different song cycles both titled Liederkreis — Op. 24 sets Heine, Op. 39 sets Eichendorff; both composed during Year of Song 1840
Composer: Toccata in C originally called 'Fantastic Study in Double Notes' — Schumann claimed it was 'the hardest piece ever written'
